design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling levels using whip mechanics and gunplay, defeat bosses, progress through increasingly varied challenges with level-specific mechanics
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Mastering hybrid combat (whip vs gun), managing escort/protection objectives, adapting to unexpected level modifiers and gameplay shifts
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Control an absurd earthworm protagonist in a robotic suit, wielding unconventional weapons and solving physics-based platforming puzzles while battling surreal antagonists
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately absurdist tone with grotesque character design (Queen Slug-for-a-Butt, Professor Monkey-for-a-Head); refusal to take itself seriously; eccentric level variety prioritizes novelty over consistency; satirical response to licensed game restrictions—first original IP from studio previously confined to corporate tie-ins

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Level select available via cheat code; invincibility mode accessible; suggests developer awareness that difficulty could be circumvented or adjusted

progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss defeats unlock next level; racing mini-game ('Andy Asteroids') gates progression with conditional boss re-fight on loss
replayability_hooks: Level-specific objectives (e.g., 'For Pete's Sake' requires protecting Peter Puppy without taking damage)
Competitive racing segments with branching outcomes

world_building_techniques: Character design as worldbuilding (grotesque anatomical humor: snot aliens, gastropod-bodied queens)
Level environment naming conveys absurdist tone ('New Junk City', level variety suggests post-apocalyptic junkyard universe)
Boss roster creates rogues gallery with distinct visual identity
Opening plot hook: earthworm finds suit; closing ironic twist (princess crushed by cow) subverts hero narrative

boss_design.philosophy: Eccentric variety over mechanical depth; each boss embodies surreal character concept; bosses serve as level variety punctuation marks
notable_bosses: Psy-Crow (appears in racing mini-game 'Andy Asteroids' and as boss)

faction_indicators: Peter Puppy (escort-protected ally in 'For Pete's Sake')
danger_signals: Grotesque enemy design signals hostile intent through body horror
Pit obstacles and environmental hazards in junkyard setting
Corrupted/mutated boss forms (organ monsters, animal hybrids) indicate systemic corruption
environmental_storytelling: New Junk City setting communicates post-industrial decay
Boss lair variety (each unique visual space) suggests distributed threat network
Suit technology as MacGuffin drives entire conflict—competing factions want it

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Earthworm Jim SNES)
